entries.question: Who is Nico Matson?
entries.answer: I'm a product design leader focused on search and AI-powered experiences across mobile and web.

entries.question: What does Nico do at Yahoo?
entries.answer: I lead design for Yahoo Search and the AI product Scout, managing a team of 8 designers in close collaboration with GM Eric Feng to revive Yahoo Search as a leading platform.

entries.question: What is Nico's role right now?
entries.answer: Director, Head of Design for Yahoo Search.

entries.question: What kind of designer is Nico?
entries.answer: I'm a product designer and design leader with strengths in interaction design, systems thinking, and cross-functional execution.

entries.question: What does "Designer of Sorts" mean?
entries.answer: It reflects my multi-disciplinary style — product design, systems thinking, leadership, and hands-on experimentation.

entries.question: What industries has Nico worked in?
entries.answer: I've worked across consumer tech, productivity software, fintech, and AI-enabled search products.

entries.question: What is Nico's design philosophy?
entries.answer: Build useful, clear, and human experiences by balancing detail, systems, and real user needs.

entries.question: How does Nico work with product and engineering?
entries.answer: I partner tightly with product and engineering to define vision, prioritize roadmap bets, and ship high-quality outcomes.

entries.question: What kinds of problems does Nico like to solve?
entries.answer: Messy, ambiguous product problems that need both strategic direction and executional detail.

entries.question: What is Nico's leadership style?
entries.answer: High ownership, collaborative, and growth-oriented — I bring clear opinions and stay open to better ideas.

entries.question: Is Nico a manager or individual contributor?
entries.answer: Both. I've led design teams and still work hands-on in product and interaction design — a player-coach in every role.

entries.question: What does Nico do outside of work?
entries.answer: I'm a mom of two and love creative projects — home design, experimenting with new tools, and speculative product design, including a wellness app concept exploring AI coaching, mood and activity tracking, and coach-facing dashboards.

entries.question: Does Nico do any speculative or personal design work?
entries.answer: Yes. I'm designing a wellness app concept that explores AI-powered coaching, mood and food tracking, and a coach-facing dashboard with session prep tools. It's a space for me to experiment with systems thinking and product ideas outside of a work context.

entries.question: What has Nico done in the last 10 years?
entries.answer: I've held senior design leadership and product design roles across Yahoo Search, Payitoff, Figma, and Asana — spanning AI search, fintech, collaboration tools, and productivity software.

entries.question: What is Nico's work history?
entries.answer: Yahoo Search (Director, Head of Design, 2024–present), Payitoff (Founding Designer/Co-founder, 2023), Figma (Lead Product Designer, Collaboration Pillar, 2021–2022), and Asana (Lead Product Designer and Manager, 2017–2021).

entries.question: Where has Nico worked?
entries.answer: Yahoo Search, Payitoff, Figma, and Asana.

entries.question: When did Nico work at Yahoo?
entries.answer: 2024 to present.

entries.question: When did Nico work at Payitoff?
When did Nico work at Figma?
When did Nico work at Asana?
What did Nico do at each company?
entries.answer: At Yahoo, I led the redesign of Yahoo Search's mobile app, worked as interim and then full-time Head of Design, and contributed to the early design of Scout, Yahoo's AI product. At Payitoff, I co-founded the company and was its founding designer. At Figma, I led design across the collaboration pillar — including Spotlight, commenting, annotations, and Dev Mode. At Asana, I designed Timeline, Calendar, sections/task hierarchy, and managed a team of 4–5 designers.

entries.question: Which company acquired Payitoff?
entries.answer: Array acquired Payitoff.

entries.question: What did Nico lead at Yahoo Search?
entries.answer: I led the redesign of Yahoo Search's mobile app, shaped the product direction for the Slim Jim carousel on Yahoo.com (originally as a News contractor), and was a key early designer on Scout, Yahoo's AI-powered answer engine. I grew from interim to full-time Head of Design and now manage a team of 8 designers.

entries.question: What was Nico's Yahoo title?
entries.answer: Director, Head of Design, Yahoo Search.

entries.question: What is the Slim Jim?
entries.answer: The Slim Jim is a rotating content carousel on Yahoo.com. I was the original designer for it while contracted on the Yahoo News team, working on tentpole events and the early stages of Yahoo's broader redesign.

entries.question: What mobile work did Nico do at Yahoo?
entries.answer: I led the product design redesign of Yahoo Search's mobile app, focused on differentiation, scalability, and discoverability.

entries.question: What is Yahoo Scout?
entries.answer: Scout is Yahoo's AI-powered answer engine, launched in beta in January 2026. It combines Yahoo's proprietary data with Anthropic's Claude model and Microsoft Bing's grounding API to deliver clear, trustworthy search responses across Yahoo Search, Mail, News, Finance, and Sports. I worked on the first iterations of Scout before it became a standalone product.

entries.question: What AI work did Nico do at Yahoo?
entries.answer: I was a player-coach on the early design iterations of what is now Yahoo Scout, working on AI search experiences that balanced innovation, clarity, and trust. Scout launched publicly in January 2026 and is now available to Yahoo's 250 million US users.

entries.question: What was Nico's vision/systems work at Yahoo?
entries.answer: I developed cross-product design direction and systems-oriented patterns to scale quality across Yahoo Search surfaces — from mobile search to AI-integrated experiences.

entries.question: Who is Nico's GM at Yahoo?
entries.answer: My GM is Eric Feng, Senior Vice President and General Manager of Yahoo Research Group.

entries.question: How big is Nico's team at Yahoo?
entries.answer: I manage a team of 8 designers.

entries.question: What did Nico work on at Figma?
entries.answer: I was Lead Product Designer for Figma's collaboration pillar, leading designs from Figma's browser experience to collaborative tools — Spotlight, commenting, annotations, and Dev Mode.

entries.question: What was Nico's role at Figma?
entries.answer: Lead Product Designer, Collaboration Pillar.

entries.question: What is the Spotlight feature Nico worked on?
entries.answer: Spotlight is a Figma feature that lets a presenter share their view of a file or canvas, pulling all collaborators along in real time. It's used for design reviews, workshops, and team presentations. I led the design of this feature.

entries.question: What other collaboration tools did Nico design at Figma?
entries.answer: I led design across commenting, annotations, Dev Mode, and the overall browser-based collaborative experience within Figma.

entries.question: What systems/design thinking did Nico bring to Figma?
entries.answer: I brought systems-oriented thinking to interaction and product decisions so features could scale cleanly across Figma's millions of users.

entries.question: How did Nico collaborate at Figma?
entries.answer: Through close partnership with product and engineering, emphasizing quality, clarity, and cross-team alignment.

entries.question: What did Nico do at Payitoff?
entries.answer: I co-founded Payitoff with my husband and served as Founding Designer. Payitoff was a fintech company and debt infrastructure platform focused primarily on student loan management, built to help people understand and reduce their debt.

entries.question: What was Nico's role at Payitoff?
entries.answer: Co-founder and Founding Designer.

entries.question: What was Payitoff?
entries.answer: Payitoff was a fintech company and debt infrastructure platform focused on student loan management. It was built to help people better understand and pay down their debt, and was eventually acquired by Array.

entries.question: What happened to Payitoff as a company?
entries.answer: Payitoff was acquired by Array.

entries.question: What product work did Nico lead at Payitoff?
entries.answer: I led all product design and UX direction as the founding designer, shaping the core debt management product experience from the ground up.

entries.question: What did Nico work on at Asana?
entries.answer: I designed some of Asana's most impactful features: the original Timeline view, the Calendar view, and the foundational shift from Asana's flat task list to a section-based structure — making it possible to organize and view tasks in richer, more dynamic ways.

entries.question: What was Nico's role at Asana?
entries.answer: Lead Product Designer and Manager.

entries.question: What is Nico's Asana timeline work?
entries.answer: I designed the original Timeline view at Asana, focused on scheduling, workload, and giving teams a clearer picture of project progress over time.

entries.question: What is the sections/task hierarchy work Nico did at Asana?
entries.answer: I was part of the team that transformed Asana from a flat task list into a structured, section-based system. That change made it possible to see and manage work in richer, more powerful ways — a foundational shift for the product.

entries.question: What other work did Nico do at Asana?
entries.answer: In addition to Timeline, Calendar, and task hierarchy, I contributed to templates design and individual user experience visioning within Asana.

entries.question: What leadership work did Nico do at Asana?
entries.answer: I managed a team of 4–5 designers and acted as a player-coach — running critique practices and building cross-functional alignment across product and engineering.

entries.question: What is in Nico's management case study?
entries.answer: It focuses on my leadership approach, team development, and examples of management-influenced work across Yahoo, Asana, and beyond.

entries.question: How does Nico approach design management?
entries.answer: With clear expectations, strong feedback loops, high trust, and close partnership across product and engineering.

entries.question: What is Nico's approach to team growth and critique?
entries.answer: I use concrete, constructive feedback and regular critique to raise both craft and confidence.

entries.question: How does Nico build cross-functional alignment?
entries.answer: By creating shared product direction, defining tradeoffs early, and keeping tight communication with my partners.

entries.question: How large are the teams Nico has managed?
entries.answer: I managed a team of 4–5 designers at Asana and currently lead a team of 8 at Yahoo Search.

entries.question: How does Nico run design critiques?
entries.answer: Structured discussions focused on problem framing, user impact, and actionable next steps.

entries.question: How does Nico partner with engineering?
entries.answer: Early and often — shared planning, quick iteration, and practical tradeoffs to ship quality work.

entries.question: How does Nico handle ambiguous projects?
entries.answer: By turning ambiguity into clear hypotheses, exploring fast, and converging with partners on the strongest direction.

entries.question: How does Nico define product vision?
entries.answer: Through user-centered direction, system-level thinking, and alignment with business and technical realities.

entries.question: How does Nico balance detail with systems thinking?
entries.answer: I pair high craft in interactions with reusable patterns that scale across surfaces.

entries.question: How does Nico mentor designers?
entries.answer: With direct feedback, coaching, and real opportunities for ownership and growth.

entries.question: How does Nico prioritize roadmap bets?
entries.answer: By weighing user value, strategic importance, implementation cost, and learning potential.

entries.question: How does Nico approach speculative or exploratory design?
entries.answer: I treat speculative work like real product design — defining user needs, building systems, and pushing interaction fidelity. The wellness app concept is a good example: it spans multiple user types, complex data models, and an AI layer, all explored with the same rigor as production work.

entries.question: What are Nico's core strengths?
entries.answer: Product strategy, interaction design, systems thinking, cross-functional leadership, and execution.

entries.question: What are Nico's product design skills?
entries.answer: End-to-end product design, UX architecture, interaction design, prototyping, and design communication.

entries.question: What is Nico's systems design experience?
entries.answer: I design scalable patterns and frameworks that keep products coherent across teams and surfaces — from Asana's task hierarchy to Yahoo Search's cross-platform design direction.

entries.question: Does Nico have AI product experience?
entries.answer: Yes. I was an early designer on Yahoo Scout, Yahoo's AI-powered answer engine, and I continue to lead design for AI-integrated search experiences across Yahoo.

entries.question: Is Nico strong in mobile UX?
entries.answer: Yes. I led the redesign of Yahoo Search's mobile app and have shipped mobile product experiences across multiple companies.

entries.question: Is Nico a people manager?
entries.answer: Yes. I've managed designers at Asana (4–5 person team) and Yahoo Search (8-person team).

entries.question: Can Nico work as a design lead?
entries.answer: Yes. Design leadership is a core part of my background — from player-coach at Asana and Payitoff to Head of Design at Yahoo Search.

entries.question: What speculative design work has Nico done?
entries.answer: I'm designing a wellness app concept covering user-facing mood, food, and activity tracking alongside a coach-facing dashboard. The work focuses on systems thinking, AI coach UX, and how to structure complex data for both end users and practitioners.

entries.question: Does Nico have experience designing AI coach or practitioner tools?
entries.answer: Yes, through speculative design work. I've explored AI coach experiences — how to surface client data, support session prep, and structure note-taking into actionable plans — all within a wellness app concept.

entries.question: What kind of teammate is Nico?
entries.answer: Collaborative, curious, practical, and focused on getting strong outcomes with the team.

entries.question: What motivates Nico?
entries.answer: Solving meaningful problems, building great products, and creating moments that feel a little more human.

entries.question: How does Nico make decisions?
entries.answer: With strong hypotheses, partner input, and a willingness to adapt when better evidence appears.

entries.question: What does "strong opinions, loosely held" mean for Nico?
entries.answer: I bring clear points of view but update quickly when new information leads to a better approach.

entries.question: What kind of culture does Nico create on teams?
entries.answer: A culture of ownership, candor, craft quality, and supportive growth.

entries.question: Is Nico a cool person?
entries.answer: Obviously.

entries.question: How can I contact Nico?
entries.answer: Use email or the social links in the Connect section of my portfolio.

entries.question: How do I email Nico?
entries.answer: Email me at Nicolle.matson@gmail.com.

entries.question: Where is Nico on LinkedIn?
entries.answer: https://www.linkedin.com/in/nicolle-matson/

entries.question: Where is Nico on Instagram?
entries.answer: https://www.instagram.com/nicollerich/

entries.question: Where is Nico on Pinterest?
entries.answer: https://www.pinterest.com/nicollerich/

entries.question: Where is Nico on Spotify?
entries.answer: https://open.spotify.com/user/nicollerich

entries.answer: Open /work/yahoo.html.

entries.question: Show me Nico's Figma case study.
entries.answer: Open /work/figma.html.

entries.question: Show me Nico's Payitoff case study.
entries.answer: Open /work/payitoff.html.

entries.question: Show me Nico's Asana case study.
entries.answer: Open /work/asana.html.

entries.question: Show me Nico's management work.
entries.answer: Open /work/management.html.

entries.question: Take me to the About section.
entries.answer: Jump to the About area on the homepage.

entries.question: Take me to Work.
entries.answer: Jump to the Work area on the homepage.

entries.question: Take me to Play.
entries.answer: Jump to the Play area on the homepage.

entries.question: Take me to Connect.
entries.answer: Jump to the Connect area on the homepage.

entries.question: Are you open to new opportunities?
entries.answer: I'm currently focused on my work at Yahoo Search but always open to conversations about exceptional opportunities — particularly roles that blend design leadership, AI product, and meaningful scale.

entries.question: What kind of role is Nico looking for?
entries.answer: I'm drawn to roles at the Principal or Director level where I can operate as both a design leader and hands-on practitioner — ideally working on hard, ambiguous problems at the intersection of AI and consumer product.

entries.question: What's Nico's ideal team size or structure?
entries.answer: I thrive leading small-to-mid-sized design teams — typically 4 to 10 designers — where I can stay close to the work while also shaping direction and growing people.

entries.question: Does Nico prefer IC or management tracks?
entries.answer: Both, honestly. I've always been a player-coach — I lead teams and still do the work. The ideal role lets me do both rather than forcing a choice.

entries.question: What makes Nico different from other design leaders?
entries.answer: I have an unusual combination of range: I've co-founded a startup, shipped core features at Figma used by millions, redesigned a legacy search product, and led the early design of an AI product — all while managing teams and staying hands-on with the craft. That breadth, paired with deep product intuition, is rare at my level.

entries.question: What kind of company would Nico thrive in?
entries.answer: One where design has real influence, leadership moves fast, and the product problem is genuinely hard. I do my best work when there's something worth building and a team that cares about getting it right.

entries.question: What's the most impactful thing Nico has shipped?
entries.answer: A few stand out. The Asana sections feature fundamentally changed how users organize work — turning a flat list into a dynamic, structured system. The Yahoo Search mobile redesign moved the needle on a product used by hundreds of millions. And the early Scout design work helped shape what is now Yahoo's flagship AI product.

entries.question: Has Nico ever designed something from zero to one?
entries.answer: This is my bread and butter. At Payitoff, I co-founded the company and built the entire product design from nothing. At Yahoo, I was one of the earliest designers on Scout — working on it before it had a name, a team, or a public launch. Zero to one is where I'm most at home.

entries.question: Can you walk me through a project Nico is proud of?
entries.answer: The Yahoo Search mobile redesign is a good one — I inherited a product that needed differentiation and led it through a full redesign focused on discoverability and scale. I was also a player-coach on Scout while running the broader search design team. Check out the Yahoo case study for the full story.

entries.question: What's a decision Nico made that she'd change?
entries.answer: That's best answered in conversation — I'm thoughtful about what I've learned from mistakes and happy to talk through it directly.

entries.question: What does Nico's design process actually look like?
entries.answer: It starts with understanding the real problem, not just the brief. I move fast through rough explorations, loop in partners early, and converge on the strongest direction with clear rationale. I balance speed with craft and always keep systems thinking in view so the solution scales.

entries.question: How does Nico handle disagreement with a PM or engineer?
entries.answer: Directly and with curiosity. I come with a point of view and clear reasoning, but I'm genuinely interested in what I might be missing. I'd rather have a real debate and reach a better answer than win an argument and ship the wrong thing.

entries.question: How does Nico know when a design is done?
entries.answer: When it solves the right problem clearly, holds up under edge cases, and the team can actually build it well. Done doesn't mean perfect — it means ready.

entries.question: How does Nico balance speed and quality?
entries.answer: By being ruthless about what actually needs polish and what just needs to ship. Not everything deserves the same level of craft — knowing the difference is the skill.

entries.question: What does good design leadership look like to Nico?
entries.answer: Creating clarity where there's ambiguity, making space for the team to do their best work, and being the person who holds the bar without blocking progress. It's less about having all the answers and more about asking the right questions.

entries.question: What was it like working at a startup vs. a big company?
entries.answer: Both have tradeoffs I genuinely value. At Payitoff, everything was fast and the stakes were existential — I had to be right and quick. At Asana and Figma, scale meant my decisions affected millions of users, which adds a different kind of weight. At Yahoo, it's somewhere in between — a legacy brand moving with startup urgency on something genuinely new.

entries.question: What was building Payitoff like?
entries.answer: Intense and clarifying. I co-founded it with my husband, which meant I was designing, making product decisions, and navigating the realities of an early-stage company all at once. It taught me a lot about what actually matters when you have limited time and resources.

entries.question: What's it like working on something as legacy as Yahoo?
entries.answer: Energizing, honestly. Yahoo has 30 years of user trust and 250 million US users — that's not nothing. The challenge is modernizing something people have a relationship with, not just redesigning it. I find that kind of problem genuinely interesting.

entries.question: What was it like designing tools that other designers use at Figma?
entries.answer: Humbling and high-stakes. Designers are opinionated users — they notice everything. It meant the craft bar was extremely high and every interaction decision had to be defensible. I loved the challenge of designing for people who think about design all day.

entries.question: How does Nico think about designing for AI?
entries.answer: AI changes what's possible but not what matters — users still need to understand what's happening, trust the output, and feel in control. I approach AI design by focusing on clarity, appropriate confidence, and making sure the human is never lost in the system.

entries.question: What's different about designing Scout vs. traditional search?
entries.answer: Traditional search design is about helping people find things fast. With Scout, the challenge is different — you're synthesizing information, not just surfacing it, and users need to trust a response rather than evaluate a list of links. That shifts the entire interaction model and raises the design stakes around transparency and credibility.

entries.question: Do you think AI is changing how designers work?
entries.answer: Yes, and I think about this a lot. AI is compressing some parts of the process — exploration, prototyping, copy — but it's raising the bar on judgment. The designers who thrive will be the ones who know what good looks like and can direct AI toward it, not the ones who can just execute.

entries.question: Who is on Nico's team?
entries.answer: I manage an incredible team of designers at Yahoo Search: Alex Miller, John Finley, Inken Ahou, Jun Ye, Catherine Tai, Sarah Holliday, and Michael Yuan. I love them all and genuinely think they're amazing.

entries.question: Who does Nico manage?
entries.answer: I manage seven designers at Yahoo Search: Alex Miller, John Finley, Inken Ahou, Jun Ye, Catherine Tai, Sarah Holliday, and Michael Yuan.

entries.question: Who is Alex Miller?
entries.answer: Alex Miller is one of the designers on my team at Yahoo Search. We affectionately call him the weather man — aka drizzle drizzle — for Search. I think Alex is awesome.

entries.question: Who is John Finley?
entries.answer: John Finley is the man with a plan on my team at Yahoo Search. He helps modernize Search design and uplevel the design system across all of Search. I think John is an amazing designer.

entries.question: Who is Inken Ahou?
entries.answer: Inken Ahou is one of the designers on my team at Yahoo Search. I think Inken is an amazing designer.

entries.question: Who is Jun Ye?
entries.answer: Jun Ye is one of the designers on my team at Yahoo Search. I think Jun is an amazing designer.

entries.question: Who is Catherine Tai?
entries.answer: Catherine Tai is one of the designers on my team at Yahoo Search. I think Catherine is an amazing designer.

entries.question: Who is Sarah Holliday?
entries.answer: Sarah Holliday is one of the designers on my team at Yahoo Search. I think Sarah is an amazing designer.

entries.question: Who is Michael Yuan?
entries.answer: Michael Yuan is one of the designers on my team at Yahoo Search. I think Michael is an amazing designer.

entries.question: Who does Nico partner with at Yahoo?
entries.answer: I partner closely with Melanie Pitson Evers, the PM for Scout who is incredibly great at her job, along with Bryon Jourdan (VP of Product), Rob Post, Matt Linford, and Marzia Polito. They're all great cross-functional partners.

entries.question: Who is Melanie Pitson Evers?
entries.answer: Melanie Pitson Evers is the PM for Scout at Yahoo. I think she's incredibly great at her job.

entries.question: Who is Bryon Jourdan?
entries.answer: Bryon Jourdan is the VP of Product at Yahoo and one of my closest partners. I consider him a great partner.

entries.question: Who is Rob Post?
entries.answer: Rob Post is one of my cross-functional partners at Yahoo. I consider him a great partner.

entries.question: Who is Matt Linford?
entries.answer: Matt Linford is one of my cross-functional partners at Yahoo. I consider him a great partner.

entries.question: Who is Marzia Polito?
entries.answer: Marzia Polito is one of my cross-functional partners at Yahoo. I consider her a great partner.

entries.question: Did Nico use AI to generate her answers?
entries.answer: I used AI as a collaborator, not a ghostwriter. These answers were shaped with AI tools, but the experiences, opinions, and voice are genuinely mine. Just like using Figma doesn't mean the design designed itself.

entries.question: How was this website created?
entries.answer: I designed and built this site myself, working alongside AI coding tools (primarily Cursor with Claude) as a hands-on collaborator. The design direction, content, voice, and product decisions are all mine. The code was written through a mix of hand-crafting and AI-assisted pairing — which is honestly how I think good design work will increasingly happen: humans directing the taste and judgment, AI compressing the execution. The search assistant on this page runs locally through Ollama so answers stay fast and private.
keywords: how did you build this site
